title: What is Python?
overview: Python is one of the most popular and beginner-friendly programming languages. In this first lecture you will learn what programming is, why Python is the perfect language to start with, and how to run your very first Python program.

blocks.title: What Does It Mean to Program?
blocks.body: **Programming** is the art of giving instructions to a computer so it can perform tasks automatically. Instead of clicking buttons in an app, you write the instructions yourself. The computer follows them step by step, exactly as written.
bullets: Computers are very fast but also very literal — they do exactly what you tell them
Programming lets you automate repetitive tasks
Programs are written in a **programming language** like Python, which both humans and computers can understand
Every program is just a sequence of instructions stored in a text file

blocks.title: Think of a Program as a Cooking Recipe
blocks.text: A recipe lists ingredients and steps: chop onions, boil water, cook for 10 minutes. A program does the same thing — it lists steps like 'store this value,' 'add these two numbers,' 'print the result.' The computer follows the recipe just like you follow cooking instructions.

blocks.title: Think Of A Program As A Cooking Recipe

blocks.question: Is it true that a programming language is like a recipe because both have a list of steps that must be followed exactly?
blocks.answer: Yes! Both recipes and programs are sequences of instructions. A recipe tells a cook what to do step by step. A program tells the computer what to do step by step. If you skip a step in a recipe, the dish may not turn out right. If you skip a step in code, the program may crash or give the wrong output.

blocks.title: Programming Languages
blocks.body: There are hundreds of programming languages, each designed for different purposes. Python is special because it is designed to be easy to read and write.
bullets: Python's syntax (the rules for writing code) is similar to English
You can write simple programs with just a few lines of code
Python hides complex details so you can focus on solving problems

blocks.title: Printing Multiple Lines
blocks.code:
print("Welcome to Python!")
print("I am learning to code.")
print("This is fun!")
blocks.expectedOutput:
Welcome to Python!
I am learning to code.
This is fun!

blocks.question: What do you think happens if you put numbers inside print() instead of text? For example: print(42)
blocks.answer: It works! Numbers don't need quotes. Python will print exactly the number you give it. Try it: print(42) prints 42.

blocks.title: Print Your Name
blocks.instructions: Write a program that prints your name on the first line and your college name on the second line.
blocks.starterCode:
# Print your name
print("")
# Print your college
blocks.hint: Use print("YourName") for the first line and print("YourCollege") for the second. Put quotes around text.

blocks.title: Why Python is Perfect for Beginners
blocks.body: Python was created by Guido van Rossum in 1991 with a clear philosophy: make programming easy and fun. Today it is used by beginners and professionals alike.
bullets: Easy to read — Python code looks almost like plain English
No complicated symbols or punctuation — clean and minimal
Huge community — millions of tutorials and libraries available for free
Runs everywhere — Windows, Mac, Linux, even on small devices

blocks.title: Real-World Applications of Python
blocks.body: Python is not just for learning — it powers some of the most important technology in the world.
bullets: **Web Development** — Instagram, Pinterest, and Spotify use Python for their backends
**Data Science** — Python is the #1 language for analyzing data and building charts
**Artificial Intelligence** — Most AI and machine learning libraries are written in Python
**Automation** — Python can automate boring tasks like renaming files or sending emails

blocks.title: Think of Python as a Swiss Army Knife
blocks.text: A Swiss Army knife has many tools — blade, scissors, screwdriver — all in one device. Python is the same: you can build websites, analyze data, make games, or automate tasks, all with the same language.

blocks.title: Think Of Python As A Swiss Army Knife

centerPanel.label: Programming Concept
centerPanel.textContent:
Python is a multi-purpose language:
• Web Development
• Data Science
• AI / Machine Learning
• Automation

rightPanel.label: Code Example
rightPanel.code:
# Python can build anything
print('Hello from Python!')

blocks.question: Can you build a website with Python even though we only printed text?
blocks.answer: Absolutely! Printing text is just the beginning. Python has special libraries (Django, Flask) that help build websites, and libraries (Pandas, NumPy) for data science. The Swiss Army Knife idea means one language can do all of these things — you just pick the right tool (library) for the job.

blocks.question: Think about something you do every day on your phone or computer. Can you imagine how a Python program could automate or improve it?
blocks.answer: There are so many possibilities! Maybe a program that automatically organizes your downloads folder, or sends you a weather alert each morning. That's the power of programming — you can build tools that make your life easier.

blocks.title: Installing Python
blocks.body: Before you can write Python programs, you need to install Python on your computer. It's free and takes just a few minutes.
bullets: Go to python.org and download the latest version
Run the installer and check 'Add Python to PATH'
After installation, open Terminal (Mac/Linux) or Command Prompt (Windows)
Type python --version to verify it installed correctly

blocks.title: Think Of Installing Python As Setting Up A Kitchen

centerPanel.label: Programming Concept
centerPanel.textContent:
Install once, use anywhere.
1. Download from python.org
2. Run installer
3. Verify with python --version

blocks.question: Do you need to reinstall Python every time you want to write a new program?
blocks.answer: No! You install Python once, and it stays on your computer. After that, you can write and run any number of programs — just like a kitchen stays ready for you to cook again and again.

blocks.title: Python Shell (Interactive Mode)
blocks.body: The **Python shell** (also called REPL) lets you type Python commands one at a time and see results immediately.
bullets: Type python in your terminal to start the shell
You'll see >>> prompt — Python is waiting for your command
Type print("Hello") and press Enter — Python runs it instantly
Type exit() to leave the shell

blocks.title: Running .py Files
blocks.body: For real programs, you save your code in a file with a .py extension and run the entire file at once.
bullets: Create a new file called hello.py
Write your Python code in the file using any text editor
Open terminal, navigate to the folder containing the file
Type python hello.py and press Enter — Python runs every line in order

leftPanel.label: Step 1
leftPanel.title: Write Code
leftPanel.description: You write Python instructions in a .py file using a text editor.

centerPanel.label: Step 2
flowSteps: Python reads your file line by line

rightPanel.label: Step 3
rightPanel.title: See Output
rightPanel.description: The result appears on your screen. If there's an error, Python shows what went wrong.

blocks.title: Interactive Shell vs File: Same Code, Same Result
blocks.code:
# In the shell:
>>> print("Hello, World!")
Hello, World!

# In a .py file:
print("Hello, World!")

# Run: python myfile.py
# Output: Hello, World!

blocks.title: Python is Interpreted
blocks.body: Python is an **interpreted language**, which means it runs your code line by line without needing a separate compilation step.
bullets: No compile step — write code and run it immediately
If Python finds an error on line 5, it stops there and shows the error message
This makes Python great for beginners — you get instant feedback
You can test small pieces of code in the shell without creating files

blocks.question: What do you think is the advantage of an interpreted language like Python compared to a compiled language?
blocks.answer: You can test code immediately without waiting for compilation. If you make a mistake, Python tells you on the spot. This makes learning faster and debugging easier.

blocks.title: Run Your First Python Program
blocks.instructions: Write a program that prints your name and your college on separate lines, then prints 'I am learning Python!' on a third line.
blocks.solution:
print("Lakshanya")
print("SRM University")
print("I am learning Python!")
blocks.hint: Use print() three times, once for each line. Each print() outputs text on a new line.

keyTakeaways.text: A program is a sequence of instructions for a computer
Python is beginner-friendly, readable, and used worldwide
print() displays text on the screen
Python is interpreted — runs line by line, no compile step
.py files store Python programs; run them with python filename.py
The Python shell lets you test code interactively

whatYouLearned: What programming is and how it works
Why Python is the best language for beginners
How to install Python and run programs
The difference between interactive shell and .py files
How to use print() to display output
